Patch Set 13:

My main concern is whether this means an interrupted layout LFSCK will never repair layouts with dangling references?  If an OSS or MDS restarts during the LFSCK, do these dangling objects still get repaired, or does LFSCK need to successfully complete on all MDTs and OSTs for this last phase of repair to be completed?

Patch Set 13:

> My main concern is whether this means an interrupted layout LFSCK will never repair layouts with dangling references? If an OSS or MDS restarts during the LFSCK, do these dangling objects still get repaired, or does LFSCK need to successfully complete on all MDTs and OSTs for this last phase of repair to be completed?


Your concern is reasonable. Because before knowing the orphans information, the layout LFSCK cannot exactly know whether the "missing" OST-object is really lost or because of LOV EA corrupted. Based on current scanning order, it is not easy to remove such dependency. But we can give the user another start option: "--force-create" or similar, if the user specify that when start the LFSCK, then the layout LFSCK will create the "lost" OST-object during the first-phase scanning, otherwise, the dangling will be handled as the patch does. How do you think?

Patch Set 15:

It looks that disk metadata on MDT, e.g. LOVEA is not trustable during LFSCK, why not checksum metadata? If so, we may avoid some wrong fixing, and the error handling can be simpler.

Patch Set 15:

> It looks that disk metadata on MDT, e.g. LOVEA is not trustable during LFSCK, why not checksum metadata? If so, we may avoid some wrong fixing, and the error handling can be simpler.

In theory, it may be a good solution. But there are some situations to be considered:
1) It only works for new created file, but not work for existed files.

2) There is no space in the LOV EA header to hold the checksum, we have to enlarge the LOV EA header. That means downgrade trouble.

3) We still need some logic in the LFSCK to handle the checksum invalid cases. For example, if the checksum calculated from the LOV EA does not matches the stored checksum, we can assume that some LOV EA slot is corrupted, but it also may be the stored checksum itself corrupted. Under such case, we still need to record the bad LOV EA slot as current patch does, and if some orphan OST-object found in the 2nd stage scanning (quite possible), then we can use it to repair the LOV EA, otherwise, we will handle it as dangling finally. So the LFSCK logic will not become more simple, instead, it needs to handle more cases.

Anyway, the checksum can be a good candidate idea in the future, not only for LOV EA, but also for kinds of Lustre metadata. Currently, without checksum, many of the LFSCK repair logic base on the assumption that something is correct, but it may be not. It is probability issue.

Andreas, what's your idea about the checksum? Thanks!

Patch Set 15:

PPC64 Builder: build succesful
Patch Set 15: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 15:

For ZFS we will already have checksums for the metadata, and in newer kernels there is also support in ext4 for metadata checksums. However, I have the same concerns about what to do if the checksum is wrong?  With ZFS this is clear - when the checksum is incorrect it will fetch the data from a different mirror copy, or rebuild it from parity. With ldiskfs, or with internal checksums it isn't clear that there is any improvement from knowing the metadata may be corrupted, since we have no other information about what the layout should be.

> For ZFS we will already have checksums for the metadata, and in newer kernels there is also support in ext4 for metadata checksums. However, I have the same concerns about what to do if the checksum is wrong?  With ZFS this is clear - when the checksum is incorrect it will fetch the data from a different mirror copy, or rebuild it from parity. With ldiskfs, or with internal checksums it isn't clear that there is any improvement from knowing the metadata may be corrupted, since we have no other information about what the layout should be.

Yes, there are still many detailed things to be considered for metadata checksum, not a simply solution. Anyway, I will update the patch to resolve merge trouble.

parents.subject: LU-6142 lnet: change wire protocol typedefs to proper structure

commit.subject: LU-8288 lfsck: handle dangling LOV EA reference
commit.message:
LU-8288 lfsck: handle dangling LOV EA reference

Originally, the layout LFSCK logic of handling dangling LOV EA
reference is as following:

During the first phase scanning, if the layout LFSCK find that
some LOV EA entry references an OST-object that does not exist,
then it will repair the inconsistency based on the LFSCK start
parameter "-c" option. If "-c" option is specified, the layout
LFSCK will think the OST-object lost, then it will create the
lost OST-object with the FID that is stored in the LOV EA slot.
But such repairing may be incorrect. Because the LOV EA may be
corrupted as to the LOV EA is invalid. Means the OST-object is
still on the OST. When moves to the second stage scaning, the
layout LFSCK will find the orphan OST-object that claims to be
as one of the MDT-object's stripe. And if someone has already
modified the new created OST-object before finding the orphan,
then the layout LFSCK cannot recover the original data back.

To avoid above trouble, the patch introduces new start option:
"--delay-create-ostobj" or short described as "-d". It allows
the layout LFSCK to postpone creating the "lost" OST-object
until all the orphan OST-objects handled. It will record the
dangling references in some new introduced layout LFSCK trace
files on disk during the layout LFSCK first stage scanning,
then travel those traces file after all orphan OST-objects
handled in the second-stage scanning. The side-effect of such
option is that as long as one OST does not join the layout
LFSCK or fail to complete the scanning, then reparing dangling
LOV EA will be skipped. For a large system with a lot of OSTs,
such condition may be a bit strict. The default value is 'off'.

Signed-off-by: Fan Yong <fan.yong@intel.com>
Change-Id: Ic222e1ad20c8011aa5f41cc43171d017ab5c464f
